title: SNT-TC-1A: Examinations, Certification, and Maintaining Certification
narrationScript: Passing a course does not certify anyone. In the employer-based model, certification happens when examinations are passed, grades are computed, vision is verified, and the certifying authority signs. In this lesson we walk the whole lifecycle, from exam day to recertification, and through the unhappy paths too, interrupted service, termination, and reinstatement. Start with the examinations. The recommended practice describes three hurdles for Level One and Level Two candidates. The general examination covers the principles of the method itself, the physics, the equipment classes, the governing variables, and it is normally closed book. The specific examination is about your shop, the employer's procedures, the actual equipment models, and the specifications and acceptance criteria the candidate will work to. The practical examination is hands-on. The candidate demonstrates the test on representative specimens, and the examiner grades against a prepared checklist covering setup, calibration, scanning or processing, interpretation, and reporting. Examinations are administered and graded by the Level Three or by a designated representative, and the number of questions and the makeup of each examination are set out in the written practice, guided by the suggested minimums in the recommended practice. Vision examinations run alongside the technical ones, and they trip up more programs than any other requirement. Near-vision acuity is checked annually, typically by reading a Jaeger-type chart at a defined distance, with corrective lenses allowed as long as the correction is worn on the job. Color discrimination, the ability to distinguish the colors and shades of contrast the method depends on, is examined when the person enters the program and then at intervals the written practice defines. An expired vision examination quietly invalidates work, so mature programs track vision dates as carefully as calibration dates. Now grading. The recommended practice uses a composite grade, a simple average of the general, specific, and practical examinations, and the passing composite is commonly at least eighty percent. Because an average can hide a weak link, written practices commonly add a floor, requiring something like seventy percent on each individual examination. A candidate who fails waits, commonly thirty days or until documented additional training is complete, before reexamination. For Level Three candidates the examinations shift upward, a basic examination on certification programs, materials, and the survey of methods, a method examination at Level Three depth, and a specific examination on the employer's own documents. Many written practices accept the central certificate from the national society in place of the first two. When the grades are in and vision is current, the employer certifies. That word is precise. Certification is the employer's written testimony of qualification, and the records behind it carry the weight, the person's name, level, and method, the education, training, and experience that qualified them, examination results with dates and grades, vision results, the signature of the certifying authority, and the dates of certification and expiration. Because the employer is the certifying authority, the certification belongs to that employment. It does not transfer to the next company, though the training and experience records travel with the person and feed the next program. Certification then has to be kept alive. A technical performance evaluation is a documented look at how a certified Level One or Level Two actually performs on the job, commonly carried out annually, and an unsatisfactory evaluation triggers retraining or reexamination under the written practice. Interrupted service is the related problem, someone who has not exercised a method for longer than the written practice allows, commonly on the order of a year. The cure is defined there too, often reexamination before independent work resumes. Recertification closes the loop. Under the model in the recommended practice, Level One and Level Two personnel recertify at intervals not exceeding three years, and Level Threes at intervals not exceeding five, either by reexamination or by documented evidence of continuing satisfactory performance, whichever the written practice requires. Two final provisions matter. Termination means that when employment ends, the certification ends with it. Reinstatement is the mercy rule. A person rehired within a window the written practice defines, commonly six months, with complete records on file, can be reinstated at the former level without starting over. And remember the referenced shelf. The topical outlines that define training content and the question references that support examinations are named in the written practice along with their editions, because an audit will ask. Keep the lifecycle in your head as one picture, qualify, examine, certify, evaluate, recertify, and you will handle every variation the exam throws at you.
imagePrompt: An NDT examiner with a clipboard observes a technician performing a hands-on ultrasonic practical examination on a steel calibration block at a brightly lit test bench in an industrial training facility.

sections.heading: Three Examinations, One Decision
sections.body:
For Level I and Level II candidates, the recommended practice describes three qualification examinations, each probing a different layer of competence.

- **General** — the principles of the method: physics, equipment classes, variables, capabilities and limitations. Normally **closed book**, because it tests what the candidate carries in their head.
- **Specific** — the employer's world: the shop's procedures, the actual equipment and techniques in use, and the codes, specifications, and acceptance criteria the candidate will work to. Built by the program, for the program.
- **Practical** — a hands-on demonstration on representative test specimens. The candidate sets up, calibrates, performs the examination, interprets, and reports while the examiner grades against a **prepared checklist** of defined checkpoints; specimens with known discontinuities verify that the candidate can actually find and disposition flaws.

Examinations are **administered and graded by the Level III or a designated representative**, and the written practice fixes the question counts and content makeup, guided by the recommended practice's suggested minimums. A candidate who fails an examination waits a period defined by the written practice — commonly on the order of **thirty days, or sooner with documented additional training** — before reexamination. For Level III candidates the set shifts upward: a **basic** examination (certification programs, materials and processes, method survey), a **method** examination at Level III depth, and a **specific** examination on the employer's documents; many written practices accept a current central (ASNT) certificate in lieu of the first two.

sections.heading: Vision: The Examinations People Forget
sections.body:
Two physiological examinations gate every certification, and lapses here invalidate more work than failed written exams ever do.

- **Near-vision acuity** — checked **annually**, typically by reading a Jaeger-type test chart at a specified distance (or an equivalent standardized test). Corrective lenses are acceptable, provided the same correction is worn during actual examinations.
- **Color discrimination** (and differentiation of shades of gray) — examined **upon entry into the program and at intervals** the written practice specifies, because penetrant indications, magnetic particle contrast, and CRT/screen presentations all depend on perceiving color and contrast differences.

The written practice states which tests, what frequency, and who administers them. A technician whose annual near-vision check has lapsed is, in audit terms, performing with an incomplete qualification — which is why disciplined programs track vision due-dates with the same rigor as instrument calibration due-dates.

sections.caption: The qualification examination structure: general, specific, and practical results combine into a composite grade, with vision examinations as a standing gate.

sections.heading: Composite Grading: How Passing Is Computed
sections.body:
The recommended practice grades Level I and Level II qualification on a **composite**: a simple average of the general, specific, and practical examination grades. The passing composite is **commonly at least 80 percent**. Because an average can conceal a weak link — a 95 on the general can drag a 62 on the practical across the line — written practices commonly add a **per-examination floor**, requiring something like **70 percent minimum on each** examination.

Work one example. A candidate scores 88 general, 74 specific, and 84 practical. The composite is (88 + 74 + 84) ÷ 3 = 82. Composite passes; no examination fell below 70; the candidate qualifies. Swap the 74 for a 64 and the composite (78.7) fails on both counts.

Two administrative points complete the picture. First, grading is the Level III's responsibility (or that of a designated representative named in the written practice), and graded papers, checklists, and answer keys are records. Second, the **number of questions** for each examination follows the written practice, which typically adopts or adapts the recommended practice's suggested minimums by method and level.

sections.title: Averages hide weak links
sections.body: A composite grade of **at least 80 percent** is the common pass mark — but a strong general score can mask a marginal practical. That is exactly why written practices add per-examination minimums (commonly **70 percent**). When you administer a program, check both numbers before you sign.

sections.heading: Certification and the Records Behind It
sections.body:
When the composite passes, the per-exam floors are met, and vision is current, the employer **certifies** — issues written testimony that the individual is qualified. The certification record is the artifact auditors actually read, and a complete one typically contains:

- name of the individual, **level, method** (and any technique limitation)
- the **education, training, and experience** that satisfied initial qualification
- **examination results**: dates, grades, and the identity of who administered and graded
- current **vision examination** results and dates
- **signature of the certifying authority** and the dates of certification and expiration

Because the employer is the certifying authority, the certification is **valid only within that employment**. It is not transferable: a new employer must certify the person under its own written practice — though the documented training and experience travel with the technician and typically shorten the path. This is the single most practical difference between employer-based certification and the central certificates we cover later in this module.

sections.caption: The certification lifecycle under the employer-based model, from qualification through recertification, with the interrupted-service and termination/reinstatement side paths.

sections.heading: Keeping It Alive: Performance Evaluation and Interrupted Service
sections.body:
Certification is not a one-time event; two mechanisms verify that qualification stays real between examinations.

**Technical performance evaluation.** Certified Level I and Level II personnel are periodically evaluated **on the job** — commonly **annually** — by documented observation of actual work: following the procedure, equipment setup, technique execution, interpretation, and reporting. This is not a re-examination; it is structured evidence that the person still performs to the standard they were certified to. An unsatisfactory evaluation triggers the written practice's remedy: supervised work, retraining, reexamination, or in serious cases revocation of the certification.

**Interrupted service.** Skills decay when unused. The written practice defines how long a person may go without performing a method — commonly on the order of **twelve months** — before action is required, and what that action is: typically reexamination, or recertification, before independent work resumes. The Level III's job is to catch interrupted service *before* the technician is put back on production work, because results produced by a lapsed qualification are exactly the kind of finding that unwinds an entire job's worth of acceptance records.

sections.heading: Recertification, Termination, Reinstatement — and the Referenced Shelf
sections.body:
**Recertification.** Certifications carry expiration dates. Under the recommended practice's model, Level I and Level II personnel recertify at intervals **not exceeding three years**, and Level III at intervals **not exceeding five years** (state these as the typical model — the written practice sets the binding values). Recertification is by **reexamination** or, where the written practice allows, by documented **evidence of continuing satisfactory technical performance**. Failed vision examinations, unsatisfactory performance evaluations, or excessive interrupted service can force recertification early.

**Termination.** When employment ends — resignation, layoff, discharge — the employer-based certification **terminates with it**. The records remain, but the testimony is void because the certifying authority no longer employs the person.

**Reinstatement.** The recovery path: a person whose certification was terminated may be **reinstated at the former level without new examinations** when rehired within a window the written practice defines — **commonly six months** — provided the certification records are on file and requirements (such as vision) are current. Beyond the window, or with incomplete records, the person re-enters through the normal qualification path.

**Referenced publications.** The recommended practice points to companion documents — the ASNT **topical outlines** that define training content and the **question references** that support examination construction. A disciplined written practice cites which referenced documents and **editions** it uses, because exam content, training syllabi, and audits all key to a specific edition.

interactive.title: You Are the Certifying Level III
interactive.intro: You administer a fabrication shop's program under a written practice based on SNT-TC-1A. The written practice requires: composite grade of at least 80 percent with no individual examination below 70; annual near-vision examinations; reexamination after interrupted service longer than twelve months; and reinstatement without reexamination when a former employee returns within six months with complete records on file. Make the calls.

steps.prompt: A Level II UT candidate scores 92 on the general, 71 on the specific, and 84 on the practical examination. What is your determination?
options: Fail — the specific grade is too close to the limit to be comfortable
Pass — the composite is about 82 and no examination fell below 70
Average only the two best scores, which gives 88, and pass
Order an immediate retake of the specific examination before deciding
steps.feedback: The composite is (92 + 71 + 84) ÷ 3 ≈ 82.3, which meets the 80 minimum, and every individual grade cleared the 70 floor. 'Close to the limit' is not a criterion — the written practice's stated rules are. Apply them as written, both ways.

steps.prompt: Scheduling shows a Level II's annual near-vision examination expired two months ago. She is assigned to evaluate production welds this afternoon.
options: Let her work — visual acuity rarely changes meaningfully in two months
Assign a Level I to double-check her accept/reject calls today
Remove her from examination duties until she passes a near-vision examination
Terminate her certification and restart her qualification from the beginning
steps.feedback: A current near-vision examination is a standing condition of the qualification. Until it is restored, she should not perform or evaluate examinations — but termination is not required, and a Level I cannot make evaluation calls at all. Administer the vision test, document it, and return her to work.

steps.prompt: A Level II radiographer returns to the NDT department after fourteen months on a non-NDT assignment. His certification has not yet reached its expiration date.
options: Resume independent work immediately — the certification is unexpired
Apply the interrupted-service provision: reexamine before he works independently
Demote him to trainee and repeat all initial training hours
Have him shadow another Level II for one shift, then resume
steps.feedback: Fourteen months exceeds the written practice's twelve-month interrupted-service limit, so its stated remedy — reexamination before independent work — governs, even though the certificate date is unexpired. An unexpired certificate does not override a lapsed condition.

steps.prompt: A Level II PT technician resigned in March and is rehired four months later. Her complete certification records are on file and her vision examinations are still current.
options: Reinstate her at her former level per the written practice, without full reexamination
Restart her as a trainee since the certification terminated
Administer all three qualification examinations again before any work
Accept her certification as never having lapsed, since she came back
steps.feedback: Her certification did terminate with employment — but the written practice's reinstatement provision covers exactly this case: returned within six months, records on file, conditions current. Reinstate, document the reinstatement, and put her to work. Without that provision (or beyond the window), reexamination would be required.

checkQuestions.question: Which qualification examination covers the employer's own procedures, equipment, and the specifications the candidate will work to?
options: The general examination
The specific examination
The practical examination
The basic examination
checkQuestions.explanation: The specific examination targets the employer's procedures, equipment, techniques, and applicable specifications; the general examination covers method principles, and the practical is the hands-on demonstration.

checkQuestions.question: A written practice requires a composite of at least 80 with no examination below 70. A candidate scores 95 general, 68 specific, 88 practical. The result is:
options: Pass — the composite of about 83.7 exceeds 80
Pass — only the composite matters under the recommended practice
Fail — one examination fell below the 70 floor despite the passing composite
Incomplete — the practical must be retaken to confirm the high grade
checkQuestions.explanation: The composite (83.7) passes, but the written practice's per-examination minimum of 70 was not met on the specific examination (68), so the candidate fails and follows the reexamination provisions.

checkQuestions.question: Under the typical written practice, near-vision acuity and color discrimination are examined, respectively:
options: Both once, at initial certification only
Annually for near vision; upon entry and at defined intervals for color discrimination
Every five years for near vision; annually for color discrimination
Only when the technician reports a problem
checkQuestions.explanation: Near-vision acuity (Jaeger-type or equivalent) is commonly checked annually, while color and gray-shade discrimination is examined on entry into the program and at intervals the written practice defines.

checkQuestions.question: What is a technical performance evaluation?
options: A written retest of method theory administered every five years
A documented, periodic (commonly annual) evaluation of a certified technician's actual on-the-job performance
The practical examination given at initial certification
An equipment calibration audit performed by the quality manager
checkQuestions.explanation: The technical performance evaluation is structured, documented observation of how a certified Level I or II actually performs work — commonly annual — with unsatisfactory results triggering the written practice's remedies.

checkQuestions.question: A certified Level II leaves the company and is rehired eight months later. The written practice allows reinstatement within six months. What applies?
options: Automatic reinstatement, since his records are still on file
Reinstatement is no longer available within the written practice's window, so he requalifies through examinations
His certification never terminated because he returned to the same employer
He may work as a Level II while studying for reexamination
checkQuestions.explanation: Employer-based certification terminated with employment, and eight months exceeds the six-month reinstatement window — so the written practice's normal qualification/reexamination path applies before he can be recertified.

references: CP-105-2024 Basic Topical Outline §1.1.8–1.1.15 (examinations through referenced publications)
SNT-TC-1A, Recommended Practice for Personnel Qualification and Certification in Nondestructive Testing (requirements summarized, current edition)
ASNT topical outlines and examination question references (summarized)
